text: The surgeon places three robotic arms and an assistant arm in the right upper quadrant for the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs check scoping using the robotic camera and instruments, followed by activating the firefly mode with indocyanine green dye to visually delineate the biliary anatomy.
The robotic camera provides a clear visualization of the duodenum, common bile duct, neck, and cystic region of the gallbladder.
The surgeon begins dissection at the neck of the gallbladder by incising the peritoneal fold, extending the incision both anteriorly and posteriorly while maintaining proximity to the liver junction.
The surgeon extends the incision as high as feasible, maintaining a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon shifts focus to dissecting within Calot's triangle, aiming to free the cystic duct and cystic artery from peritoneal attachments and surrounding fibro-fatty tissue.

The surgeon has created a plane between the cystic duct and cystic artery. The cystic artery is positioned superiorly, while the cystic duct is located inferiorly, clearly visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the firefly mode multiple times to accurately identify the anatomy, highlighting the cystic artery. An aberrant vessel is visible extending into segment four, and the decision is made to preserve this vessel.
The surgeon frees the cystic duct from surrounding fibro-fatty tissue, ensuring that the area is clear for snug placement of surgical clips.
The surgeon uses ICG and Firefly mode to illuminate and define the biliary anatomy, ensuring precise visualization for secure clipping of the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects to preserve the aberrant vessel encountered near the cystic artery and duct. Following this, the cystic artery and cystic duct are clipped securely.
The surgeon uses the Firefly mode to reconfirm the biliary anatomy. Two clips are applied distally and one proximally on the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic scissors to divide the cystic duct and cystic artery, completing the separation of these structures.
The surgeon carefully divides the aberrant vessel, ensuring it is protected during the procedure.

The surgeon frees the gallbladder from its attachments at the liver bed, performing half of the dissection before using the camera flip technique to enhance visual clarity and complete the remaining dissection.
The surgical field is viewed using a thirty-degree upward angle, providing an equivalent perspective to that used in laparoscopic surgery.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the aberrant vessel, carefully separating it from the liver and gallbladder, ensuring its preservation.
The surgeon carefully and slowly dissects the gallbladder away from the liver bed, encountering loose areolar tissue and some inflammation due to acute calculus cholecystitis.
